stem: A permutation \( \sigma \) is a product of two disjoint cycles, one of length [[a]] and one of length [[b]]. What is the minimum number of transpositions (2-cycles) whose product equals \( \sigma \)?
solution: A cycle of length \( \ell \) is a product of \( \ell - 1 \) transpositions, and disjoint cycles add, so \( \sigma \) needs \( ([[a]] - 1) + ([[b]] - 1) = [[answer]] \) transpositions.
distractor_rationales: This adds the cycle lengths without subtracting one per cycle.
This multiplies \( (a-1)(b-1) \) instead of adding the transposition counts.
This is off by one from the minimum count.
This is off by one from the minimum count.
This is off by two from the minimum count.
This is off by three from the minimum count.

stem: A single cycle \( \sigma = (c_1\; c_2\; \cdots\; c_{[[k]]}) \) has length [[k]]. What is the minimum number of transpositions needed to write \( \sigma \) as their product?
solution: A \( k \)-cycle decomposes as \( (c_1\;c_2\;\cdots\;c_k) = (c_1\;c_k)(c_1\;c_{k-1})\cdots(c_1\;c_2) \), a product of exactly \( k - 1 \) transpositions, and no shorter decomposition exists.
distractor_rationales: This mistakes the cycle length itself for the transposition count.
This overshoots by one beyond the correct minimum.
This undershoots by one below the correct minimum.
This mistakenly halves the cycle length.
This doubles the correct minimum count.
This assumes every cycle can be written as a single transposition, which is false for \( k > 2 \).

stem: A permutation \( \sigma \) is a product of two disjoint cycles, of lengths [[a]] and [[b]]. Recall the sign of a permutation is \( \operatorname{sgn}(\sigma) = (-1)^{t} \), where \( t \) is the number of transpositions in any decomposition of \( \sigma \). What is \( \operatorname{sgn}(\sigma) \) (that is, is \( \sigma \) an even or an odd permutation, reported as \( +1 \) or \( -1 \))?
solution: A cycle of length \( \ell \) contributes \( \ell - 1 \) transpositions, so \( t = ([[a]]-1)+([[b]]-1) \) here, and \( \operatorname{sgn}(\sigma) = (-1)^{t} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This flips the correct sign.
The sign of a permutation is always \( \pm 1 \), never 0.
This confuses the sign (\( \pm 1 \)) with an unrelated difference of the cycle lengths.
This confuses the sign (\( \pm 1 \)) with the sum of the cycle lengths.
This confuses the sign (\( \pm 1 \)) with the product of the cycle lengths.
This reports one cycle length itself instead of the sign.

stem: A permutation \( \sigma \) is written (not necessarily disjointly) as a product of exactly [[m]] transpositions. Since the parity of the number of transpositions in ANY decomposition of \( \sigma \) is always the same, what is \( \operatorname{sgn}(\sigma) = (-1)^{[[m]]} \)?
solution: The sign of a permutation written as a product of [[m]] transpositions is \( (-1)^{[[m]]} = [[answer]] \), regardless of how the transpositions were chosen, since parity of the count is a well-defined invariant of \( \sigma \).
distractor_rationales: This flips the correct sign.
This reports the transposition count itself instead of the sign.
The sign of a permutation is always \( \pm 1 \), never 0.
This is off by one in the exponent's parity, flipping the sign.
This is off by one in the exponent's parity, flipping the sign.
The sign of a permutation is always \( \pm 1 \), never 2.

stem: Permutation \( \sigma \) is a product of two disjoint cycles of lengths [[a]] and [[b]]; permutation \( \tau \) (on a disjoint set of points) is a product of two disjoint cycles of lengths [[c]] and [[d]]. Using the fact that \( \operatorname{sgn} \) is a homomorphism (\( \operatorname{sgn}(\sigma\tau) = \operatorname{sgn}(\sigma)\operatorname{sgn}(\tau) \)), what is \( \operatorname{sgn}(\sigma\tau) \)?
solution: \( \operatorname{sgn}(\sigma) = (-1)^{([[a]]-1)+([[b]]-1)} \) and \( \operatorname{sgn}(\tau) = (-1)^{([[c]]-1)+([[d]]-1)} \); since \( \operatorname{sgn} \) is multiplicative, \( \operatorname{sgn}(\sigma\tau) \) is their product, \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This flips the correct sign.
The sign of a permutation is always \( \pm 1 \), never 0.
This adds the raw cycle lengths instead of multiplying the two signs.
This multiplies the raw cycle lengths instead of multiplying the two signs.
This mixes the cycle lengths with the wrong signs instead of computing \( \operatorname{sgn}(\sigma)\operatorname{sgn}(\tau) \).
This subtracts unrelated cycle lengths instead of multiplying the two signs.
